content.text: A dashboard that nobody owns is a dashboard nobody trusts. When an alert fires and the on-call engineer opens the linked dashboard only to find stale queries, missing labels, or panels with no data, the dashboard actively harms incident response. Ownership standards prevent this: they assign clear responsibility for each dashboard's accuracy, define what a review must check before merge, and establish a lifecycle (draft → active → deprecated) that keeps the dashboard inventory honest over time.

attrs.alt: Dashboard ownership lifecycle and review checklist overview

content.text: Without ownership, dashboards accumulate debt silently:
Metric renames break queries — nobody notices because nobody is responsible for the dashboard.
Alert thresholds become incorrect after scaling — the dashboard shows the old value.
New team members inherit dashboards from departing colleagues with no handoff.
Duplicate dashboards for the same service accumulate, confusing on-call responders.
An ownership model solves this with two rules: every dashboard has exactly one owner, and that owner is responsible for keeping it accurate and reviewing changes.
Grafana's JSON model allows arbitrary tags. Use a consistent tag vocabulary to make ownership machine-readable and queryable:

// In every managed dashboard definition
dashboard.new(
  title='Claude — Golden Signals',
  uid='claude-golden-signals',
  tags=[
    'claude',              // subject area
    'observability',       // category
    'owner:platform-team', // owning team (slug from your org directory)
    'oncall:sre-squad',    // team paged when alerts on this dashboard fire
    'reviewed:2025-06-01', // date of last ownership review
    'alerts:yes',          // whether alerts link back to this dashboard
    'runbook:https://wiki.example.com/runbooks/claude-golden-signals',
  ],
  ...
)
prefix convention lets you query Grafana for all dashboards owned by a given team:

# List all dashboards owned by platform-team
curl -sS "$GRAFANA_URL/api/search?tag=owner:platform-team" \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $GRAFANA_TOKEN" | jq '.[].title'
Run this query as part of an ownership audit script to find dashboards with missing owner tags, stale review dates, or departing team names:
GitHub CODEOWNERS ensures that dashboard PRs are automatically routed to the right reviewer — the team that owns the affected dashboards:

# .github/CODEOWNERS (in the observability repository)

# Platform team owns the shared library and infrastructure dashboards
dashboards/lib/                @org/platform-team
dashboards/infrastructure/     @org/platform-team

# Claude-specific dashboards are owned by the AI platform team
dashboards/claude/golden_signals.jsonnet   @org/ai-platform
dashboards/claude/cost_overview.jsonnet    @org/ai-platform @org/product-eng
dashboards/claude/quality_signals.jsonnet  @org/ai-platform @org/feature-squads

# Any new dashboards without explicit owners fall to platform team
dashboards/                    @org/platform-team
With CODEOWNERS in place, a PR touching
before it can merge. This creates a lightweight gate without requiring manual reviewer assignment.
Every dashboard PR must pass this checklist before merge. Add it as a pull request template so contributors see it automatically:

<!-- .github/PULL_REQUEST_TEMPLATE/dashboard.md -->
## Dashboard PR Checklist

### Required
- [ ] `uid` is set, unique, and follows the naming convention (`<subject>-<purpose>`)
- [ ] `editable: false` is set (managed dashboards are read-only in the UI)
- [ ] `owner:<team-slug>` tag is present
- [ ] `reviewed:<YYYY-MM-DD>` tag is updated to today
- [ ] `runbook:<url>` tag links to the ops runbook for this service
- [ ] All panel PromQL expressions are non-empty and have been tested against a live Prometheus
- [ ] `refresh` interval is set (not empty string)
- [ ] Time range is not hardcoded (uses relative like `now-3h`)

### Recommended
- [ ] Threshold lines are set on time-series panels that correspond to alert rules
- [ ] A text panel at the top describes what the dashboard shows and who it is for
- [ ] Cross-dashboard links are present (e.g. cost panel links to golden signals)
- [ ] Variable templating (`$feature`, `$model`) is used instead of hardcoded label values
- [ ] `rendered/` JSON is committed alongside the `.jsonnet` source

### If removing a panel
- [ ] Confirm no alert rule references the removed panel by ID
- [ ] Check whether any runbook links to the panel directly
Ownership is not a one-time assignment — it needs a quarterly refresh to catch:
Team restructures that made the assigned owner tag stale.
Metrics that were renamed or deprecated since the last review.
Panels that consistently show "No Data" because the metric was never actually instrumented.
Dashboards that duplicate each other because two teams built the same thing independently.
Run the ownership audit script and send results to team leads monthly, with a 30-day resolution deadline:

# .github/workflows/dashboard_audit.yaml
name: Dashboard Ownership Audit

on:
  schedule:
    - cron: '0 9 1 * *'  # 1st of each month at 9am UTC
  workflow_dispatch:

jobs:
  audit:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Run ownership audit
        env:
          GRAFANA_URL: ${{ secrets.GRAFANA_URL }}
          GRAFANA_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.GRAFANA_API_TOKEN }}
        run: python3 scripts/audit_dashboard_ownership.py > /tmp/audit.txt 2>&1 || true

      - name: Open GitHub issue if issues found
        if: ${{ hashFiles('/tmp/audit.txt') != '' }}
        env:
          GH_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
        run: |
          ISSUES=$(cat /tmp/audit.txt)
          if echo "$ISSUES" | grep -q "\["; then
            gh issue create \
              --title "Monthly dashboard ownership audit — $(date +%Y-%m)" \
              --body "$ISSUES" \
              --label "observability,ownership"
          fi
Removing a dashboard incorrectly breaks alert links and runbook references. Follow a structured deprecation process:
Grafana folder — it remains accessible for 30 days.
tag and a text panel at the top with a deprecation notice and a link to the replacement dashboard.
Update any alert rules that link to this dashboard's UID to point to the replacement.
After 30 days, open a PR to delete the Jsonnet source from the repository.
The CI pipeline removes the dashboard from Grafana when the deletion PR merges.
Move to Archive folder + add deprecation notice
Notify teams that link to the dashboard
An orphaned dashboard is one where the
tag points to a team that no longer exists, or where the owning team member has left. The audit script surfaces these as
Identify whether the dashboard is still used — check Grafana's view count (Admin → Dashboard stats).
If it has had no views in 90 days, treat it as a candidate for immediate deprecation.
If it is actively viewed, assign it to the team most likely responsible (the team that owns the monitored service).
Contact the new owner to confirm they accept responsibility and will update the tag and review date.
If no team claims it within two weeks, the platform team takes ownership and evaluates whether the dashboard should be kept.
Never delete a dashboard without first checking whether any alert rule or runbook links to it by UID. A broken alert link during an incident is worse than a stale dashboard.
Beyond ownership, maintain a minimum quality bar for all dashboards in the managed folder:
Every panel shows data in the last 7 days
Visual inspection or Grafana dashboard health API
All $variable references have corresponding template definitions
jsonnet render — will error on undefined vars
Time-series panels tied to alerts show threshold lines
At least one text or markdown panel explains the dashboard purpose
UIDs never change after first deploy
schemaVersion matches current Grafana major version

Add automated tests for these standards in the CI pipeline (see the testing section of the GitOps workflow document). Structural checks — UID presence, editable flag, non-empty queries — can be encoded as pytest assertions against the rendered JSON. Visual checks — panels show data — require a live Prometheus and are better handled by the quarterly review process.
Consistent naming across dashboards speeds up navigation during incidents. Apply these conventions to every managed dashboard:
Lowercase slug matching the repository subject
One of: reliability, cost, quality, business, debug

UIDs are the most critical naming element because they appear in alert links, runbook references, and Grafana API calls. Once a UID is deployed to production, it must never change — treat it as a permanent identifier. If a dashboard is fundamentally restructured, create a new UID, redirect old links to the new one, and deprecate the old dashboard.
Team Onboarding: Getting Ownership Right from Day One

New teams integrating Claude into their product frequently ask whether they need to create custom dashboards or can use the standard suite. The answer is: start with the standard suite, add feature-specific panels as needed.
Copy the standard dashboard templates (golden_signals, cost_overview, quality_signals) and set the
File a PR with your team's CODEOWNERS entry added.
Run the ownership audit to confirm your dashboards appear with correct metadata.
Attend a quarterly dashboard review after your first 90 days in production.
Document your feature-specific metrics in your team's runbook and link from the dashboard description panel.
Teams that skip this onboarding step typically inherit orphaned dashboards from previous owners six months later. Starting correctly is far cheaper than fixing it retrospectively.

Dashboard ownership standards convert dashboards from ad-hoc artifacts into maintained, accountable operational assets. The key elements are: an owner tag on every dashboard, CODEOWNERS routing PRs to the right reviewers, a PR checklist that gates merge on structural correctness, a monthly audit that surfaces orphaned and stale dashboards, and a deprecation process that prevents broken links during incidents.
Encode ownership, review date, and runbook links as structured tags in the dashboard JSON.
Use CODEOWNERS to auto-assign PR reviewers based on which dashboard file changed.
Run a monthly audit script that flags missing owners, stale review dates, and orphaned dashboards.
Follow a 30-day deprecation process before deleting any dashboard to protect alert and runbook links.
Automate structural quality checks in CI; handle data-quality checks in quarterly reviews.
